digest.lead: Anthropic rolled out 1M context windows for Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 to all users today, marking a major milestone in making extended reasoning accessible across all plan tiers.
digest.summary: Claude Code also shipped two significant updates with MCP elicitation support and sparse checkout features, while the community continues building tools for prompt optimization and cost savings.

stories.headline: 1M Context Windows Now Available for All Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 Users
stories.summary: Anthropic has made 1M context windows generally available for Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 across all plan tiers [1]. The announcement generated significant discussion with nearly 1,000 upvotes and 388 comments on Hacker News [1].
key_points: Previously, extended context required additional usage fees
Available immediately for Max, Team, and Enterprise plans
Represents a major accessibility improvement for long-form reasoning tasks
stories.impact: This democratizes access to extended reasoning capabilities for all Claude users, particularly benefiting researchers, writers, and analysts who work with lengthy documents or complex codebases.

sources.title: 1M context is now generally available for Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6

stories.headline: Claude Code Adds MCP Elicitation Support and Sparse Checkout Features
stories.summary: Claude Code v2.1.76 introduced MCP elicitation support, allowing servers to request structured input mid-task through interactive dialogs [1]. The update also includes sparse checkout support for large monorepos and new session naming features [1][2].
key_points: New Elicitation and ElicitationResult hooks enable mid-task input collection
Sparse checkout via `worktree.sparsePaths` optimizes large repository workflows
Session naming and color coding improve workflow organization
stories.impact: These updates significantly enhance Claude Code's capabilities for complex development workflows, particularly benefiting teams working with large codebases who need better repository management and interactive task flows.

stories.headline: Developer Criticizes A/B Testing Impact on Claude Workflows
stories.summary: A blog post titled "Anthropic, Do Not A/B Test My Workflow" gained traction on Hacker News, highlighting concerns about how experimental changes affect established development workflows [1].
stories.impact: Reflects growing concerns among power users about workflow stability as Claude becomes more integral to daily development practices.

sources.title: Anthropic, Do Not A/B Test My Workflow

stories.headline: New Tool Promises 90% Token Savings Through Auto-Caching
stories.summary: A community-built tool called prompt-caching.ai automatically injects Anthropic cache breakpoints to reduce token usage by up to 90% [1]. The project gained attention on Hacker News with developers discussing optimization strategies.
key_points: Automatically identifies optimal cache breakpoint placement
Designed specifically for Anthropic's prompt caching system
stories.impact: Addresses a key pain point for developers and organizations with high Claude usage, potentially making advanced AI workflows more cost-effective for smaller teams and individual developers.

sources.title: Prompt-caching – auto-injects Anthropic cache breakpoints (90% token savings)

stories.headline: Write a CLAUDE.md file to set project conventions once
stories.summary: Adding a CLAUDE.md to your project root lets you define coding standards, test commands, and architectural decisions that persist across every Claude Code session. Think of it as onboarding documentation that Claude reads automatically — you teach it once instead of repeating yourself.
actionable_steps: Create a CLAUDE.md in your project root with build/test/lint commands
Add coding conventions like naming patterns, preferred libraries, and file structure
Include architectural decisions and constraints Claude should respect
Use /init in Claude Code to auto-generate a starter CLAUDE.md

stories.headline: Use @-mentions to give Claude Code precise file context
stories.summary: Boris Cherny, creator of Claude Code, recommends using @-mentions to point Claude at specific files instead of letting it search. This dramatically reduces token usage and improves accuracy since Claude works with the exact files you intend rather than guessing from project structure.
actionable_steps: Type @ in Claude Code to see a file picker with fuzzy search
Select the specific files relevant to your task before writing your prompt
Combine multiple @-mentions for cross-file tasks like refactoring

stories.headline: Use Claude's extended thinking for complex architectural decisions
stories.summary: When asking Claude to make non-trivial design choices — like choosing between patterns, structuring a new module, or planning a migration — enabling extended thinking produces significantly better results. Claude will reason through trade-offs before committing to an approach, catching issues that quick responses miss.
actionable_steps: For architecture questions, ask Claude to 'think through the trade-offs' explicitly
Use Claude Code's plan mode (shift+tab) for multi-step tasks that need careful reasoning
Review Claude's reasoning in extended thinking to catch flawed assumptions early

sources.title: Tips for getting better architectural advice from Claude
